nameMeanings.name: Ambrose
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and Scottish: from the personal name Ambrose (French Ambroise, Latin Ambrosius, from Greek ambrosios ‘immortal, divine’), which was popular throughout Christendom in medieval Europe. Its popularity was due in part to the fame of Saint Ambrose (c. 340–397), one of the four Latin Fathers of the Church, the teacher of Saint Augustine. In North America, the English form of the surname has absorbed cognates from other languages, e.g. Czech, Slovenian, and Croatian Ambrož (see Ambroz ), and also their derivatives, e.g. the Slovenian patronymic Ambrožič.
Irish: from Mac Ambróis ‘son of Ambrose’ (see 1 above); a West Munster name, which has also been Anglicized as McCambridge .

variants.name: Broggi
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Aldo, Domenic, Lorenzo, Umberto.
Italian: from a short form of the personal name Ambrogio,Italian form of Ambrose .

variants.name: Ambroise
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Pierre, Emile, Jean Robert, Serge, Dominique, Fernande, Herve, Jacques, Jean Baptiste, Lucien, Mathurin, Yva.
French and West Indian (mainly Haiti): from the personal name Ambroise, an equivalent of Ambrose . In the US, the surname is apparently almost exclusively of Haitian origin.

variants.name: Brause
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Eckhard, Gerhard, Manfred, Otto.
German:
from a vernacular pet form of the personal name Ambrosius (see Ambrose ).

variants.name: Ambrogio
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Carmelo, Pasquale, Sal, Aldo, Nicola, Rocco, Salvatore, Sebastiano.  Spanish Fortunato, Carlos, Gaspar, Valeria.
Italian: from the personal name Ambrogio (see Ambrose ).

variants.name: Ambrosius
meanings: German and Dutch: from the Latin personal name Ambrosius (see Ambrose ), or a Latinized (humanistic) form of its German vernacular variant Ambros .

variants.name: Amberson
meanings: Americanized form of Ambrossen or Ambrosson, rare Norwegian patronymics from Ambrose .
English: variant of Emerson with intrusive -b-. This surname is very rare in Britain.

History: Abraham Amberson is listed in MD court records in 1665–80. Subsequently the name is found among Irish Presbyterians in Amberson Valley, PA, in 1763. Norwegian bearers of this name came to North America in the mid 19th century, possibly also earlier.

variants.name: Ambush
meanings: Possibly an altered form of   Ambrose  . This surname is found mainly among African Americans.

variants.name: Brasel
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Menachem, Mirra, Yakov.
German (Bräsel): from a shortened pet form of the Latin personal name Ambrosius (see Ambrose ).
Altered form of Jewish (from Poland) Brezel or Brezyl: artificial name from Polish brezylia ‘brazilwood’.

variants.name: Ambroz
meanings: Czech, Slovenian, and Croatian (Ambrož); Slovak (also Ambróz, rarely Ambrož): from the personal name Ambrož, Slovak Ambróz, equivalent to Ambrose . In North America, this surname may also be a shortened form of the Slovenian patronymic Ambrožič, of the same origin. Compare Ambros .

variants.name: Ambrosio
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Pedro, Mario, Andres, Cristina, Edmundo, Emilia, Erlinda, Jesus, Luis, Albino, Alejandro, Alfonso.  Italian Salvatore, Antonio, Angelo, Francesco, Marco, Romeo, Saverio, Alessio, Aniello, Biagio, Carmela, Carmelo.
Italian and Spanish: from the personal name Ambrosio (see Ambrose ).

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.
